item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images used in this article are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as they all are released into Public Domain, as seen here, here, here, here, and here.
Ayydrian Lmaudin

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Onto the fridge you go!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Until I get a decent camera this image'll have to do.

+ show block
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty great, insofar as it is obviously ambitious and doesn't make any attempt to hold the reader's hand. However, without any familiarity with the philosophy of art, the logs read more as a word salad than anything else. There was a vague ideas that I had of a narrative, but I was certain that I was missing some ultimate, deeper meaning. Reading the spoilers, I realize that I was more or less on the mark, and that the story simply did not inspire me at all.

This is a piece of good work that did not work for me at all.

Also, is it just me, are there an increasing number of articles that pay homage to/are based on Borges' writing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fanart, author of the meme

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw the title and thought "I hope that's a Borges reference".

This didn't disappoint.

+1

item.title: Good SCP
item.content:encoded: Te text is intriguing, it absorbed me and from the first paragraph, good scp, author

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like skips that delve into how anomalous rather simple exaggerations of human consciousness are, so this is a big up for me. Especially since this one so subtly implies that the truly anomalous property is simply a behavior every old normie could be capable of. It really is artfully written in of itself.

That being said, I have to agree that this feels more like a tale then a skip, with the anomalous sound feeling almost a little forced into the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't clear enough about the continuation, sorry. Loss and Nostalgia is a collection of three mini-tales each about a different article. They are only thematically linked. The part to do with Maxwellists is, in fact, a continuation to a different article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm still not getting it, even with the spoiler and the link to the 'continuation' (the addition of the Maxwellists just adds another piece that I'm not seeing how it fits into the whole).

No vote, because perhaps if I had read all the Borges I'd been intending to read, and picked up on whatever other references you have, I'd think it was brilliant. It's well-written, and intriguing, but I have no idea what's actually going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely well-written and insightful, but I definitely feel there is something I'm missing. Even with the spoilers above, it looks more like a Tale than an actual SCP. The only thing actually anomalous here is the synaesthesia-inducing sound and the references to AWCY.
No vote, because of the quality of the work and because I feel I'm simply not cultured enough to get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I debated whether or not to say anything about this one, but here is a bit of a summary of what the journals are saying.

Spoilers maybe?
Spoilers maybe?

The first important detail to notice is the footnote in the description, which refers to a recovery log for some blackboxed SCP object, implying that the art show both involved other objects that would become skips and formed some kind of incident large enough to be called the "Greenwich Incident". The disks, then, are a record of what happened before the Greenwich incident from the perspective of Adrian Baudin.

Baudin, during the weeks before the art show, has an idea to replicate famous works in order to gain a deeper appreciation for them. As he begins to do this, he realizes that the result has evolved beyond his original conception and races to expand his newfound abilities. He reasons that, as art affects people, what he's doing is unpacking the way people have been affected and recreating the stimulus. He does this first on himself, and then on others.

While this happens, Baudin has run-ins with several other groups, such as a small group of artists (whom I lovingly refer to as 'the cool kids') who ask for Baudin's help with a 'project' (as well as generally terrorizing him) and a woman on their trail with unknown motives.

As tensions between these people come to a head, Baudin decides he's reached a breakthrough and wants to teach others of his revolutionary new investigation of inspiration, on which he ruminates in the last log. He has plans to begin his new mission after the show. Of course, the show doesn't go well, the incident happens, and the Foundation cleans up the mess (if they weren't directly involved to begin with). Baudin escapes to places unknown, his work stolen.

Spoilers maybe?

I hope that helped with you and anyone else having problems with the plot of the article. It is very reliant on the philosophy and some subtle cues that not everyone might notice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somewhat interesting, but the article is just a lot of journals, and I didn't understand what they meant at all. Maybe if someone could explain it to me I'd like it more, but for now, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It isn't theoretically impossible and you only need to do it to a degree that your deposition matches the resolution of the tests for authenticity. Painting isn't magic.

Like I said this was considered to be feasible (but prohibitively expensive) in the 80s. Some of these techniques are being used for restorations now, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh? I thought…

*facepalm*

Read "younger" as "older" in that post. A few times. Hence my confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, he's saying that the error range for late 19th century dates is usually a significant percentage of estimated age (useless) and usually greater than the estimated age (worse than useless) for 20th century dates.

Worse than useless is a greater degree of uselessness than merely useless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't think you were talking about molecular placement. If you could do that, it would probably be accurate to this degree, but I don't think you can do that to nearly this scale? Like, manually arrange trillions of trillions of molecules without ever screwing up any of the others in the process. And if you could… it would be anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Erm… isn't the Industrial Age older than the Atomic Age?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you place each molecule of pigment in a position exactly matching a molecule of pigment in the original, how do you not also replicate the appearance of brushstrokes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To sirpudding:
I think the Foundaton take into account the methods, but I would also argue that it isn't quite as doable as you could expect, especially when there are many hidden layers to paintings many don't exactly consider. No matter how accurately you can "depose" paint, you can't perfectly recreate brushtrokes in three dimensions at such a scale, I think.

To sirpudding and Voct: I see now that the use of radiocarbon dating is inaccurate. Any suggestions for a method that would work in this context?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be specific: radiocarbon dating is useless on anything younger than the Industrial Age, and worse than useless on anything younger than the Atomic Age.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really, really dig this…disk six especially.
Can tell you dug deep to hit that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm trying to blissfully ignore that, thank you very much. D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I wouldn't say without even a hint. The paper discs mentioned are probably AWCY, what with

The discs make me laugh, because of how cool they are. They make me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
glad to see this got upvoted, I loved it when i read it in chat!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded, though I suspect this is an offshoot of the recent discussions on anart. I mean, if nothing else, it's anart without even the hint of AWCY, and so it feels like something an artist with an anomalous talent might actually try and do. The logs are compelling, I just can't piece everything together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written, but I don't really 'get' it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I totally dug it when I read the draft, and I totally dig it now. Nice job Ty, take your upboat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The spirit in which this is written counts for a lot. This is an exploration of the artistic impulse, but more importantly, the way that the notion comes to the artist demands the means that he uses. In my more optimistic moods, I imagine that five, ten years down the line, fiction will evolve to the point where communities like ours will produce works on a regular basis that demand acceptance as legitimate examples of the writing art. Pieces like this, which show the reader something common and relateable, but also necessarily unique and human, are a step forward in that regard.

Another thing this does well is the voice in which the character speaks. Not only does the author reach for a character a little bit out of the safety zone of most writers here, but he succeeds in making the character sound believable. Dialogue and literary voice are frequent problems around the wiki, but not in this article.

All in all, I can buy in. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are correct. There are many references interspersed throughout as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Technically, there's no difference. However, in the article "disk" is used to refer to the CDs and "disc" refers to the origami cognitohazards Baudin describes in several logs. Mostly just an artistic choice there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't it be disc, not disk? Or does it not matter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Based on Pierre Menard, Author of the Quixote, if my Borges hasn't failed me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This may be the most actually artistic anart skip I have read.

I have two small quibbles:

resemble the original paintings to an anomalous degree of accuracy

I don't think a perfect forgery is inherently anomalous. It ought to be possible to use very accurate microscopic cameras and very precise computer controlled paint deposition to do this in real life. I remember a Scientific American article about this in the 80s, surely it's even more doable today. Obviously his methods are anomalous, but I don't think that the forgeries are themselves so.

radiocarbon dating of paints

Since these are all recent 19th and 20th century paintings, radiocarbon dating isn't likely to produce accurate results anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So the first five anomalies are the guy trying to recognize what went in to some older masterpieces, while the sixth thing was him trying to utilize what he had learned in a new and anomalous manner?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thinking, analyzing, inventing … these aren’t anomalous acts, but rather the normal respiration of intelligence.

- Jorge Luis Borges

Thanks to Aleph, Minmin, Twistedgears, Athena_Grey, SnarkyPotato, Rumetzen, Kalinin, and more for reviewing my various drafts, and extra thanks to Kalinin for writing the metacritique on anomalous art that inspired me to run with my idea and get this written.

EDIT:

For a continuation, look here. Only the section labeled "2: An Artist Spends a Weekend in a Basement" is related to this article.

Expect something new on this. Soon.

EDIT 2:

Here's something new.
